content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 1,
1992
Frank Hoffman
12291 Harbor
Garden Grove,
Ca
92640
RE:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—382
Dear Mr.
Hoffman:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1
!
which regulate the appropriate use of campaign funds.
Because
your request refers to past conduct,
we treat it as one for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
!
Please
note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate
any conduct which may have already taken place.
QUESTION
Surplus funds from your unsuccessful Senate campaign were
transferred to your school board campaign.
Was the transfer an
appropriate use of surplus funds?
CONCLUSION
Once campaign funds become surplus funds,
the Act limits
their use.
Permissible use of surplus funds raised after January
1,
1989,
does not include transferring funds from your Senate
campaign to your school board campaign.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2 Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section S3114; Regulation 18329(c).)
Stcct, Sui SOO
•
P.(’).
O7
•
Swtiient.. (.-\
Q5S1 2
•


Frank Hoffman
1—92—382
Page Two
ANALYSIS
Under the court’s decision Service Employees International
Union.
AFL-CIO.
CLC. et al. v.
FPPC,
(9th Cir.
1992)
955 F.2d
1312, cert.den.
U.S.,
candidates are free to transfer campaign
funds from one of their committees to another of their committees
without restriction until the funds become “surplus” under the
Act.
Effective January 1,
1989,
campaign funds become “surplus”
and subject to certain restrictions when an officeholder leaves
office or,
in the case of a defeated candidate, at the end of the
post-election reporting period following the candidate’s defeat.
(Section 89519.)
Because the funds in your Senate campaign are
“surplus” under the Act, these funds may not.be transferred to
your school board campaign.
Under Section 89519,
surplus funds can be used only for the
following purposes:
(a)
The payment of outstanding campaign debts or
expenses related to the Senate campaign.
(b)
The pro rata repayment of contributors to the
Senate campaign.
(c)
Donations to any bona fide charitable,
educational,
civic, religious,
or similar tax—exempt, nonprofit
organization, where no substantial part of the proceeds will
have a material financial effect on the former candidate or
elected officer,
any member of his or her immediate family,
or his or her campaign treasurer.
(d)
Contributions to a political party or committee so
long as the funds are not used to make contributions in
support of or opposition to a candidate for elective office.
(e)
Contributions to support or oppose any candidate
for federal office,
any candidate for elective office in a
state other than California,
or any ballot measure.
Because you have terminated your Senate campaign bank
account,
you may expend funds from your school board account in an
amount equal to the funds you originally transferred.
You should
dispose of these funds in accordance with the provisions set out
in Section 89519 outlined above.
In addition,
you should file an
additional campaign statement for the Senate committee reflecting
the proper disposition of the surplus funds.
The Senate committee
should disclose the amount as a transfer from the school board
committee on Schedule I—Miscellaneous Increases to Cash,
and the
disposition of those funds as an expenditure on Schedule E.
The


Frank Hoffman
1—92—382
Page Two
Summary Page should also be amended to reflect these changes.
Furthermore,
a cover letter should be included explaining the
reason for the additional filing.
I hope this information has been helpful.
Please feel free
to contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Linda Moureaux
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division
